title: QCon AI Boston: Production AI Moves Beyond Prompts to Platforms, Harnesses, and Evals
gist: A Klaviyo AI engineer's recap of QCon AI Boston 2026 identifies three cross-cutting industry trends: context/agent infrastructure consolidating into a dedicated platform layer, security shifting from prompt-level guardrails to explicit 'harness' architectures with state ownership and audit trails, and AI adoption itself becoming an operating-model problem (cost attribution, tool access policy, evaluation) rather than a simple API-access rollout.

themes.theme: Context and agent infrastructure as a platform layer

insights.claim: Teams at QCon AI Boston 2026 are moving beyond single-purpose AI applications toward shared systems for context, tool access, identity, and state, with concepts like context engineering, MCP gateways, and semantic tool catalogs increasingly treated as core infrastructure requiring dedicated owners and contracts.
insights.speaker: Tatiana Fesenko
insights.so_what: Signals that MCP gateways and context-engineering tooling are consolidating into must-have enterprise infrastructure, relevant for evaluating vendors building shared agent-context platforms.
insights.quote: Context engineering isn't a feature, it's architecture. Get this right and everything else gets easier
insights.locator: Ricardo Ferreira's "Beyond Prompting: Context Engineering for Production-Grade AI"

insights.claim: OpenAI's opening keynote argued that even when the model itself is fast, there is a quiet, essential stretch of work before inference -- assembling enough context to be useful while trimming enough to stay fast -- meaning basic product engineering fundamentals remain critical infrastructure work even as raw model speed improves.
insights.speaker: Tatiana Fesenko
insights.so_what: Counters a narrative that faster models alone solve production AI performance -- context assembly/trimming engineering remains a persistent cost center regardless of model speed gains.
insights.locator: Martin Spier's "Keeping ChatGPT Fast as AI Development Accelerates"

themes.theme: Trust shifts from prompts to harness architecture

insights.claim: Security for AI agents is shifting from prompt-level guardrails toward a 'harness' -- the system around the model that enforces clear ownership of state, ordered writes, approval boundaries, and a real audit trail -- because a tool can execute actions the user never sees, so the operative question becomes whether the system can prove what action was taken, by which component, under which constraints and privileges.
insights.speaker: Tatiana Fesenko
insights.so_what: Reframes AI-agent security investment priorities away from prompt-engineering safeguards toward auditable execution infrastructure -- relevant for evaluating agent-security and observability vendors.
insights.quote: Own the state. Order the mutation. Prove the action
insights.locator: Vinoth Govindarajan's "The Agent Harness: Control Planes, Invariants, and Approval Boundaries for Production AI Agents"

themes.theme: AI adoption as an operating-model problem

insights.claim: Once AI usage spreads inside an organization, exposing a model via an API or handing engineers a chatbot proves insufficient -- teams instead need paved paths, shared policy surfaces, evaluation loops, observability, cost attribution, and feedback mechanisms that make the correct behavior easier than the risky shortcut.
insights.speaker: Tatiana Fesenko
insights.so_what: Frames enterprise AI adoption maturity as an operating-model and governance challenge rather than a technology-access problem, relevant to how enterprises should budget AI platform investment beyond model licensing.
insights.quote: Write strategy early. Build around customers. Own company-fit surfaces
insights.locator: Siddharth Kodwani and Swaroop Chitlur's "Building GenAI Platform at DoorDash"

insights.claim: Single-turn tests and static benchmarks are described as a weak fit for AI agents that use tools, maintain state, carry context, and behave differently across turns -- evaluation methodology needs to shift toward conversations, traces, simulations, and production feedback, or benchmarks will report success while users hit failures the test never exercised.
insights.speaker: Tatiana Fesenko
insights.so_what: A direct critique of prevailing AI-agent benchmark methodology, relevant to how investors and enterprises should discount vendor-reported agent-benchmark scores that rely on single-turn or static test designs.
insights.locator: A standout topic is how engineering organizations should think about evaluation

insights.claim: The conference's overall framing is that production AI is becoming less about prompt engineering and more a systems problem spanning context, data contracts, LLM/MCP gateways, state, evaluation, latency, cost, observability, and security -- with agents behaving like coworkers conversationally but failing like software operationally, meaning platform-engineering and distributed-systems discipline, not new AI-specific magic, is what makes them reliable.
insights.speaker: Tatiana Fesenko
insights.so_what: A synthesizing industry-wide thesis: value capture in production AI is shifting toward infrastructure/platform-engineering competence rather than prompt-crafting skill, relevant to talent and tooling investment decisions.
insights.quote: Agents may talk like coworkers, but they fail like software, and operating them well depends on old lessons from platform engineering and distributed systems.
